title: Cretaceous Legged Snake Najash rionegrina: Implications for Snake Evolution and the Phylogenetic History of the Jugal Bone
summary: A remarkably preserved fossil of Najash rionegrina from Argentina, dating to the Late Cretaceous (~100 Ma), provides critical insights into the early evolution of snakes. The specimen exhibits hind limbs and a well-developed jugal bone (cheekbone), a feature that has been lost in modern snakes. Using micro-computed tomography (micro-CT), researchers reconstructed the skull in exceptional detail, revealing neurovascular pathways and previously obscured bones. This study, published in Science Advances in 2019, challenges the prevailing hypothesis that snakes evolved from small, burrowing ancestors. Instead, the evidence supports a large-bodied, wide-mouthed ancestral morphology. The research also clarifies a long-standing anatomical debate: for 160 years, the jugal bone in snakes and related lizards was misidentified. The Najash fossils provide empirical evidence to correct this error, demonstrating a gradual loss of bones leading to the specialized skull of modern snakes. The study highlights that early snakes retained hindlimbs for an extended period before the evolution of limbless forms. Subsequent research, including the 2020 description of Boipeba tayasuensis (a large blind snake from Brazil) and a 2023 study reconstructing brain morphology of fossil and extant squamates, supports the complexity of snake origins. A 2025 study on a Middle Jurassic squamate from Scotland further indicates that early squamate evolution involved considerable anatomical experimentation and convergent evolution. These findings collectively underscore that snake evolution was not a linear progression but a complex process with multiple adaptive pathways.

background_read: Snake evolution has been debated for decades, with competing hypotheses regarding their origin: burrowing vs. aquatic vs. terrestrial. The discovery of legged snakes like Najash provides crucial transitional fossils. The jugal bone is part of the temporal region of the skull and its presence or absence is important for phylogenetic studies. Gondwana was a southern supercontinent that fragmented during the Mesozoic, and many early snake fossils are found in former Gondwanan regions. Convergent evolution is common in squamates, making phylogenetic inference challenging.
Article_Structure: Main Points: The Najash fossil provides empirical evidence for large-bodied, wide-mouthed ancestors of modern snakes and corrects a 160-year-old misidentification of the jugal bone. Purpose: To refine the evolutionary history of snakes and demonstrate the utility of micro-CT in paleontology. Evidence Evaluation: High-resolution micro-CT data provides robust anatomical evidence; the fossil is well-preserved and complete enough for detailed analysis. Author Credibility: The authors are affiliated with reputable institutions (Universidad Maimónides, University of Alberta) and have expertise in paleontology and anatomy. Methodology: Micro-CT scanning, 3D reconstruction, comparative anatomy with extant and fossil squamates. Critical Assessment: The study is limited by the single specimen but offers strong evidence; subsequent studies have both supported and complicated the findings, indicating complex evolution.

perspectives.description: View the Najash fossil as a key piece in understanding snake evolution, particularly regarding skull morphology and limb retention. It underscores the importance of detailed fossil analysis.
Interested in the genetic and developmental mechanisms underlying limb loss and skull modification in snakes. The fossil provides a temporal framework for such changes.
Use this discovery to teach about the nature of science, how hypotheses are tested, and how new evidence can overturn long-held beliefs.
